evidence: We may incorporate traditional and generative AI solutions into our business, and these solutions may become important in our operations over time.
We may pursue certain of those technologies and consumer offerings if we believe they offer a sustainable customer proposition and can be successfully integrated into our business model.
We may incorporate traditional and generative AI solutions into our business, and these solutions may become important in our operations over time.
The use of these AI solutions may expose us to additional risks and expenses, including, for example, if we were to incorporate AI technologies which we become dependent on.
The continued rise in AI technologies may intensify our cybersecurity risks.
The rapid evolution and continued increased adoption of AI technologies amplifies these concerns.

social media and the rise of artificial intelligence-generated content has dramatically increased the rate at which negative publicity...can be disseminated before there is any meaningful opportunity to respond.

limitations: All excerpts are from risk_factors section only; no business, MD&A, or capex sections are present, so investment intensity cannot be scored above 2 — no named systems, vendors, dollar amounts, or dedicated AI budgets are disclosed.
AI narrative is entirely aspirational/conditional ('may incorporate', 'may become important'); no deployed use cases or quantified outcomes are described.

Risk disclosure names specific risk categories (cybersecurity amplification, reputational harm via AI-generated content, dependency risk, adoption timing risk) but provides no quantification or named governance/oversight bodies, capping score at 3.
